To find the number of practice sessions that maximizes the average batting score, we analyze the quadratic equation \( S = 50 + 5n - 0.2n^2 \). This is a downward-opening parabola where the maximum value occurs at the vertex. The vertex formula for \( n \) in \( an^2 + bn + c \) is \( n = -b / (2a) \). Here, \( a = -0.2 \) and \( b = 5 \). Calculating the value gives \( n = -5 / (2 \times -0.2) = -5 / -0.4 = 12.5 \). Since practice sessions must be whole numbers, we test integers 12 and 13. For \( n = 12 \), \( S = 50 + 60 - 28.8 = 81.2 \). For \( n = 13 \), \( S = 50 + 65 - 33.8 = 81.2 \). Both yield the same maximum score of 81.2 runs. Thus, 12 or 13 sessions maximize the score.

Next, we calculate the win probability change when sessions increase from 5 to 10. First, determine the score at \( n = 5 \): \( S_5 = 50 + 25 - 0.2(25) = 70 \). Then, determine the score at \( n = 10 \): \( S_{10} = 50 + 50 - 0.2(100) = 80 \). Now apply the logistic function \( P = 1 / (1 + e^{-(0.1S - 3)}) \). For \( S_5 = 70 \), the exponent is \( -(0.1(70) - 3) = -(7 - 3) = -4 \). So \( P_5 = 1 / (1 + e^{-4}) \approx 1 / (1 + 0.0183) \approx 0.9821 \).
